XM97iScAAAAJ.keywords: Computer Vision, Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Computer Graphics, Robotics, Texture Synthesis, Video Generation, Facial Expression Recognition, Human Activity Recognition, Ubiquitous Computing
XM97iScAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Irfan Essa is a **Distinguished Professor of Computing** at the Georgia Institute of Technology and a **Research Scientist** at Google. He leads and contributes to research groups that span vision, graphics, robotics, and ubiquitous computing (including Georgia Tech’s *Future Computing Environments* and Living Laboratories efforts) and collaborates broadly on industry–academia projects. His work centers on **Computer Vision**, **Artificial Intelligence**, and **Machine Learning**, with a strong emphasis on practical systems for *video and image synthesis*, *human activity and facial behavior analysis*, and *embodied perception and interaction*. Across decades of work he has advanced methods for <u>example-based texture and video synthesis</u>, <u>robust facial and human activity modeling</u>, and <u>perception for interactive and ubiquitous environments</u>—and more recently has contributed to large-scale generative and embodied AI systems.

---

## Research Areas
Essa’s research covers the intersection of visual perception, generative modeling, and interactive environments. Early foundational work developed robust systems for observing and modeling faces and facial actions (e.g., “A vision system for observing and extracting facial action parameters,” 1994; “Coding, analysis, interpretation, and recognition of facial expressions,” 1997), and for model-based head and eye tracking. In visual synthesis and graphics he introduced practical, patch-based and optimization-driven approaches to texture and video synthesis (“Graphcut textures,” 2003; “Texture optimization for example-based synthesis,” 2005; “Video textures,” 2023), and developed scalable spatiotemporal segmentation techniques for long video sequences (“Efficient hierarchical graph-based video segmentation,” 2010). In ubiquitous computing and assistive environments he helped define and evaluate living-lab approaches for sensing and behavior-aware homes (“The aware home,” 1999; “Ubiquitous sensing for smart and aware environments,” 2002), and applied wearable and ambient sensing to activity discovery and health-related tasks (e.g., food intake detection, eating recognition). More recent contributions extend to low-shot and one-shot dense prediction (“One-shot learning for semantic segmentation,” 2017), self-supervised and contrastive representations for human activity recognition, and large-scale generative video and embodied agents (DD-PPO for navigation; VideoPoet and MAGVIT for video generation), reflecting a trajectory from geometric and statistical vision toward deep, multimodal, and embodied AI.

---

## Notable Works
- "Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts" (2003) — introduced a practical patch-based synthesis method that stitches transformed patches along optimal seams to produce high-quality image and video textures; this work established techniques widely used in example-based synthesis and collage-style editing.
- "The aware home: A living laboratory for ubiquitous computing research" (1999) — defined a research paradigm and infrastructure for deploying sensing, perception, and human-centered experiments in instrumented home environments, catalyzing long-term research in assistive and pervasive computing.
- "Coding, analysis, interpretation, and recognition of facial expressions" (1997) — presented a robust vision pipeline combining optical flow, geometric and physical models to produce reliable parametric representations of facial motion, substantially advancing automated facial expression analysis.

---

## Academic Background
Irfan Essa has a long-standing academic career at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he holds the title of **Distinguished Professor of Computing** and has been a driving force behind Georgia Tech’s research on living laboratories and the Future Computing Environments (FCE) group. He also maintains an active research role in industry as a **Research Scientist at Google**, reflecting sustained collaboration across academia and practice. Essa’s publication record spans foundational work in face modeling and action recognition in the 1990s, major contributions to texture and video synthesis and spatiotemporal segmentation in the 2000s, and leadership in modern deep-learning and embodied AI topics in the 2010s–2020s (including low-shot segmentation, large-scale RL for navigation, and multimodal generative video models). His scholarly impact is substantial (over 29,000 citations across his corpus), and he has mentored numerous students and led interdisciplinary teams across computer vision, graphics, robotics, and ubiquitous computing. For more information, see his homepage: http://www.irfanessa.com/.

Y67rbX0AAAAJ.keywords: Human-Computer Interaction, Culturally Relevent CS Education, AI Education, Middle School CS Education, Teacher Professional Development, Assessment of AI Knowledge, Place-based Education, Hawaiian Language Immersion, Generative AI Educational Technology, Battery-free Computing
Y67rbX0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
William Gelder is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, embedded in the university's **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)** and computing-education communities (affiliated with the School of Interactive Computing and related research groups). His work centers on designing and evaluating educational technologies and curricula that support *meaningful, equitable learning* about computing and artificial intelligence for young learners and their teachers. Across projects he pursues <u>AI education</u>, <u>culturally relevant CS pedagogy</u>, and <u>sustainable ubiquitous computing</u> as interconnected research strands, bringing together participatory design with teachers and communities to produce practical classroom materials and evaluation methods.

---

## Research Areas
Gelder's research spans several overlapping areas in HCI and computing education. He investigates design and co-design processes for middle-school AI curricula and teacher professional development (e.g., designing a 9‑week AI elective and PD in partnership with school districts and the Georgia Department of Education). He develops and studies place‑based, culturally responsive educational technologies for language-immersion and Indigenous-context classrooms (work with Hawaiian Kaiapuni schools explores *assets-based approaches* to teacher support and generative educational systems). In parallel he explores sustainable and embedded computing platforms (batteryless Arduino systems and code-transformation techniques) that enable low-resource, environmentally conscious deployments of interactive technologies. Finally, he studies assessment and evaluation strategies for K–12 AI knowledge, focusing on age-appropriate evaluation instruments and classroom‑scale measures of both technical and ethical understanding.

---

## Notable Works
- "From lecture hall to homeroom: co-designing an AI elective with middle school CS teachers" (2025, 5 citations) — co-designed a 9‑week middle‑school elective and associated teacher PD, documenting iterative teacher-centered design practices for introducing AI concepts in formal K–12 settings.
- "" Those don't work for us": An Assets-Based Approach to Incorporating Emerging Technologies in Viable Hawaiian Teacher Support Tools for Culturally Relevant CS Education" (2024, 4 citations) — articulates an assets‑based, place‑sensitive approach to designing teacher tools for Hawaiian bilingual immersion (Kaiapuni) contexts, emphasizing cultural continuity and teacher readiness.
- "User-directed Assembly Code Transformations Enabling Efficient Batteryless Arduino Applications" (2024, 4 citations) — presents methods for transforming low-level code to enable more efficient, battery‑free Arduino deployments, contributing to sustainable ubiquitous computing platforms suitable for educational and community settings.

---

## Academic Background
Gelder's publication record indicates an interdisciplinary trajectory bridging **HCI**, **computing education**, and *sustainable embedded systems*. His collaborative projects involve partnerships with multiple universities, the Georgia Department of Education, K–12 school districts, and Hawaiian immersion schools—reflecting a strong emphasis on applied, community‑engaged research. With a body of work focused on co‑design, curriculum development, tool creation, and assessment, Gelder is positioned within Georgia Tech's ecosystem of HCI and computing-education scholarship. His work to date (total citations: 18) highlights an emerging scholar establishing a portfolio of practitioner-facing studies, design artifacts, and evaluative methods that support equitable, place‑based computing education.
